title: SEGMENT 7 — Integration: how the provirus joins your genome forever

lines.text: SEGMENT 7 — Integration: how the provirus joins your genome forever
The product of reverse transcription is a linear double-stranded DNA with LTRs on both ends.
Sitting in the cytoplasm.
Bound by a complex of viral proteins — RT, integrase, and some others — collectively called the pre-integration complex, or PIC.
The PIC has two jobs.
One, get to the nucleus.
Two, splice the viral DNA into a host chromosome.
Getting to the nucleus is non-trivial.
The nucleus, in an interphase cell, is bounded by the nuclear envelope, and the only way in is through nuclear pore complexes — which are gates with active import machinery.
Most macromolecules need a nuclear localization signal recognized by importins to get through.
Here we see a major split in the retrovirus family.
The gammaretroviruses, like murine leukemia virus, cannot cross an intact nuclear envelope.
They have to wait for the cell to enter mitosis, at which point the nuclear envelope breaks down and the PIC has free access to chromatin.
This is why gammaretroviral vectors only efficiently transduce dividing cells — it's a major limitation in gene therapy.
The lentiviruses, which include HIV-1, can cross intact nuclear envelopes.
The capsid itself, with associated proteins, docks at the nuclear pore and uses cellular import machinery, including importin-7 and a host factor called CPSF6, to traverse the pore.

This is enormously important biologically — HIV infects resting CD4 T cells and terminally differentiated macrophages, neither of which divide much — and it's the central reason lentiviral vectors have replaced gammaretroviral vectors for most gene therapy applications.
You can transduce neurons, hematopoietic stem cells, post-mitotic cells, all because of this property.
Once the viral DNA is in the nucleus, integrase goes to work.
The chemistry is two reactions.
3'-end processing.
Integrase cleaves two nucleotides off the 3' ends of each strand of the viral DNA.
This generates recessed 3'-OH groups at both ends, with characteristic CA overhangs.
This happens in the cytoplasm before nuclear entry, actually.
Strand transfer.

Inside the nucleus, integrase finds a stretch of host DNA, makes a staggered cut in the host duplex, and uses the 3'-OH ends of the viral DNA to attack the host backbone in a transesterification reaction.
The viral DNA's 3' ends become covalently joined to the host DNA's 5' ends, on opposite strands of the host.
The intermediate has the viral DNA flanked by short single-stranded gaps where the staggered cut was made.
Host repair enzymes then fill in the gaps and seal the nicks, generating the characteristic target site duplication of about 4 to 6 base pairs flanking the integrated provirus.
The result is a viral DNA permanently inserted into a host chromosome.
We call this the provirus.
It is, for all practical purposes, now a Mendelian piece of the host genome.
It will be replicated by cellular DNA polymerase every time the cell divides.
It will be transcribed by cellular RNA polymerase II.
It is, in a real sense, part of the host.
Now, here's a thing worth knowing for gene therapy and oncogenesis: integration is not entirely random.
Different retroviruses have different integration site preferences.
HIV-1 strongly favors integration into the bodies of actively transcribed genes.
Murine leukemia virus favors transcription start sites and active enhancers.
Avian sarcoma virus is much more random.
These biases come from interactions between integrase and host chromatin factors — for HIV, integrase binds a host factor called LEDGF/p75 that tethers it near active genes.
This matters clinically because if a viral vector integrates near a proto-oncogene and turns it on, you can transform the cell.
In the early 2000s, four out of about twenty children treated for X-SCID with a gammaretroviral vector developed leukemia because the vector kept integrating near the LMO2 proto-oncogene.
This led to the development of self-inactivating lentiviral vectors and continued careful integration-site profiling in modern gene therapy trials.
We'll come back to this in segment 16.
